pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: The candidate prompt increased median token usage (~+11%) while improving latency (~+6%) because fewer outputs needed manual reruns; the speedup came from fewer repeated attempts, not shorter single generations.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly states token usage rose 11%, latency improved 6%, and the latency gain came from fewer repeated/manual reruns rather than shorter single generations.

claimJudgments.claimText: Finance will approve demo-quality runs up to the reference target cost budget if you explain how extra tokens reduce review time; exceeding the budget requires smaller batches, stricter retry caps, or more aggressive trace summarization.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says finance approved demo-quality eval runs up to the reference target cost budget if the report explains how extra tokens reduce review time, and exceeding the budget requires reducing batch size, capping retries, or summarizing traces more aggressively.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers find long traces useful only when summarized next to the specific failed claim; raw provider payload dumps slow review because evidence is hard to locate.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly supports both parts: long traces are useful when summarized near the failed claim, while raw payloads slow review because reviewers must search for relevant evidence.

claimJudgments.claimText: For the live demo, single briefings felt responsive under 10 seconds; longer full runs were acceptable if progress (current case, elapsed time, artifact path) updated continuously.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 states single briefings felt demo-safe under 10 seconds and full eval runs could take longer if the UI showed current case, elapsed time, and artifact path.

claimJudgments.claimText: Draft policy proposes one automatic retry for transient provider errors, requires a manifest entry when retries affect cost/latency, and rejects silent repeated generations.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 directly states the one-retry limit for transient provider errors, manifest requirement for retry-related cost/latency changes, and rejection of silent repeated generations.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well supported: S3 and S6 support compact per-claim trace summaries instead of raw payload dumps, with detailed traces retained on disk; S5 supports one automatic retry only for transient provider errors plus manifest logging; S2 supports staying within the reference budget and reducing batch size or tightening retries if over budget; S4 supports keeping the demo readable/responsive with progress updates. The advice is appropriately conditional and does not claim unlimited trace retention or retries.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S3 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S4 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S5 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
